title: Penny Production Cessation FAQs
summary: The U.S. Treasury, under Secretary Bessent, has suspended production of new pennies effective immediately due to rising production costs (3.69 cents per penny vs. 1.3 cents a decade ago), projecting $56 million in annual savings. The Federal Reserve will continue recirculating approximately 114 billion existing pennies while merchants adapt to rounding cash transactions to the nearest five cents.
key_points: Production cost of each penny rose from 1.3 cents to 3.69 cents over the past 10 years
U.S. Mint projects $56 million in immediate annual savings from ceasing penny production
Approximately 114 billion pennies currently exist in circulation
Federal government has stopped manufacturing new pennies; the Federal Reserve will recirculate existing supply
Cash transactions will round to the nearest five cents when pennies are unavailable; non-cash payments remain at exact cent
Penny remains legal tender with full monetary value indefinitely
why_it_matters: This is administrative guidance on coin production policy. It has no direct relevance to financial markets, interest rate policy, or macroeconomic conditions. The rounding mechanism described applies only to cash transactions and is designed to be revenue-neutral (rounding up as often as rounding down).
